Effective management of the organization can significantly contribute to ensuring the effectiveness of its work. At-Risk Youth - Refers to young individuals who face circumstances and experiences that increase their vulnerability to adverse outcomes. Common risk factors include poverty, family dysfunction, substance misuse, academic struggles, and involvement in delinquency. In the context of social work, it refers to youth who have reported histories of sexual exploitation or family or relationship violence. This is particularly in the case of Liberia, where children were conscripted and made child combatants during the civil war. More fundamentally, youth are vulnerable because they are people who live in an unequal world where the social values and institutions that permit opportunities and possibilities of all kinds are not available to everyone. Civil Society Organizations - Refers to a wide array of organizations: community groups, non-governmental organizations [NGOs], labor unions, indigenous groups, charitable organizations, faith-based organizations, professional associations, and foundations.” When mobilized, civil society, sometimes called the “third sector” (after government and commerce) - has the power to influence the actions of elected policymakers and businesses. But the nature of civil society - what it is and what it does - is evolving, in response to both technological developments and more nuanced changes within societies. In Liberia, the unique role of CSOs is highly recognized and respected. In 2010, the Government of Liberia, through the Governance Commission (GC) and the Ministry of Finance and Development Planning (MFDP), developed and signed an Accord or MOU with CSOs, through the National Civil Society Council of Liberia (NSCL), that highlights and articulates the role of CSOs in Liberia. National Traditional Council - Liberia’s tradition and culture play an essential role in helping to maintain peace and stability in Liberia and forging inter-ethnic, inter- communal, and political reconciliation. In this regard, the National Traditional Council of Liberia, which comprises traditional chiefs and elders, is very indispensable to the implementation of the Liberia National Action Plan on Youth Peace and Security. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 39 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 The young people unquestionably come from various tribal and ethnic backgrounds and have respect for their cultural values and traditions. During the implementation of the NAP YPS, the NTCL, which is represented in all 15 counties of Liberia, will continue to help instill cultural values and moral principles in young people, thereby contributing to the maintenance of peace and security. The Chiefs and Elders, as they are also considered guardians of the land, can help address intergenerational issues related to land and ensure access to land for young people, including young women. Inter-Religious Council of Liberia - Formulated during the Liberian Civil War and comprises both Christian and Islamic groups in Liberia. The IRCL helped broker peace in Liberia during the civil war, and since then, this group has remained very relevant in promoting peace and security. Besides running several programs in Liberia, including schools, universities, hospitals, clinics, and health centers, religious institutions are also involved in relief and other programs. As the moral conscience of society,

text: also considered guardians of the land, can help address intergenerational issues related to land and ensure access to land for young people, including young women. Inter-Religious Council of Liberia - Formulated during the Liberian Civil War and comprises both Christian and Islamic groups in Liberia. The IRCL helped broker peace in Liberia during the civil war, and since then, this group has remained very relevant in promoting peace and security. Besides running several programs in Liberia, including schools, universities, hospitals, clinics, and health centers, religious institutions are also involved in relief and other programs. As the moral conscience of society, religious institutions have a unique role in instilling religious values and morality in the young people, most of whom are their members. In addition, most religious institutions in Liberia provide various skills and vocational training, and they also help provide jobs and employment opportunities for young people. At the same time, some of these institutions have agricultural programs, are involved in counselling and providing psychosocial support, and can become a suitable conduit for implementing the NAP YPS. Zogos (M) and Zogoes (F) – At-risk and vulnerable youths in Liberia. Out-of-School Girls – School-going age girls who are not in school. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 40 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 THEORY OF CHANGE NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 41 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Develop an if-then, hypothesis theory that will lead to the desired change As stated earlier, one of the data collection processes ascertained what, in the views of the respondents, could be perceived as the most significant change (MSC). The answers varied; nevertheless, it was still possible to deduce a Theory of Change. It follows: If the Government of Liberia (GoL) and young people, with comprehensive and sustained partnership with the UN, Bilateral and Multilateral partners, INGOs, CSOs, CBOs, Traditional Chiefs and Elders, and Religious Institutions: Strategically invest significant financial and human resources towards Youth Peace and Security (YPS) objectives; Ensure equitable access to quality education, comprehensive health services, and vital digital literacy and cybersecurity education for all young people; Foster meaningful youth representation and participation at all levels of governance and decision-making, ensuring that young women and men equally exercise agency and influence; Work to institutionalize Youth Peace and Security (YPS) frameworks and agendas across relevant sectors, ensuring their systematic integration into national planning and implementation; Implement robust protection and prevention systems to eliminate all forms of violence—including gender-based violence, sexual exploitation, domestic violence, rape, early marriage, and female genital mutilation while safeguarding youth from political violence, arbitrary detention, and degrading treatment; Effectively implement comprehensive programs for the disengagement and reintegration of at-risk and vulnerable youths, and school dropout girls; Drive comprehensive youth empowerment and sustainable economic opportunities through diverse forms of skills training, capacity building, micro-financing initiatives, and the provision of psychosocial support; And proactively develops adaptive strategies to mitigate the impacts of climate change on youth livelihoods and security, while actively combating political violence and polarization. Then Liberia will then strengthen young people's sense of agency, increase their sense of safety, improve resources, and systematically address the core systemic issues and challenges hindering its youth population. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 42 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Because Holistic well-being, equitable access to opportunities (economic, education, health), and meaningful political participation are fundamental to fostering young people's agency, empowering them to become active drivers of positive social change and sustainable development; Addressing deep-rooted issues like violence,

source_name: Liberia National Action Plan on YPS 2025 2030
text: youth livelihoods and security, while actively combating political violence and polarization. Then Liberia will then strengthen young people's sense of agency, increase their sense of safety, improve resources, and systematically address the core systemic issues and challenges hindering its youth population. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 42 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Because Holistic well-being, equitable access to opportunities (economic, education, health), and meaningful political participation are fundamental to fostering young people's agency, empowering them to become active drivers of positive social change and sustainable development; Addressing deep-rooted issues like violence, exclusion from governance, and lack of digital literacy builds trust in governing bodies and societal structures, thereby enhancing legitimacy and strengthening social cohesion is crucial for reducing polarization; The elimination of physical violence, particularly gender-based violence, is a cornerstone of peace and human security, allowing young people to thrive without fear; and Sustained resource contribution and investment specifically allocated to YPS objectives are essential to create an enabling environment and provide the necessary programs and support for young people to overcome challenges, adapt to emerging threats like climate change, and contribute fully to lasting peace and security in Liberia. Guiding Principles The following principles will underpin all aspects of this framework: Youth-Centered Approach: Young people are at the heart of this framework. Their voices, needs, and aspirations will inform all interventions. Gender Equality and Equity: The framework commits to equitable access and participation for all young people, particularly young women, persons with disabilities, rural youths, and other marginalized groups, so that no one is left behind. Participation and Empowerment: Young people will be actively involved in decision-making processes at all levels, from planning to implementation and monitoring. Conflict Sensitivity: Interventions will be designed and implemented to minimize unintended negative impacts and contribute to the promotion of peacebuilding. Sustainability: The framework will promote sustainable solutions that build local capacities and ensure long-term impact. Intergenerational Approach: Although this NAP focuses primarily on young people, its implementation should also consider various age groups within the Liberian society, such as parents and children. It will consider the relationships and interactions between them. FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Coordination The Ministry of Youth and Sports and the Ministry of Internal Affairs will co-lead the overall coordination of the NAP under the umbrella of the Liberia National Coalition on Youth Peace and Security, in collaboration with youth groups, civil society organizations (CSOs), and other national and international partners, with technical support from the United Nations, led by the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA). The Ministry of Youth and Sports (MYS) will spearhead resource mobilization with the support of the Ministry of Finance and Development Planning (MFDP). At the same time, the Ministry of Internal Affairs (MIA) will ensure policy coherence and foster local government ownership and implementation of the NAP. Pillar 1: Participation: Ensure meaningful and inclusive participation of youth in peacebuilding, governance, and decision-making at all levels. Lead Institution(s): Ministry of Youth and Sports (MoYS) Ministry of Internal Affairs Supporting Institutions: Ministry of Gender, Children and Social Protection Ministry of Education Ministry of Finance and Development Planning Federation of Liberian Youth Civil Society Organizations (CSOs) National Elections Commission NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 62 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Pillar 2: Protection: Protect young people from all forms of violence, exploitation, and abuse, including in conflict and post-conflict situations. Lead Institution(s): Ministry of Justice Supporting Institutions: Ministry of Gender, Children and Social Protection Ministry of Youth and Sports Ministry of Internal Affairs National Human Rights Commission Police and other Security Forces Liberian National Red Cross Society United Nations agencies International organizations Pillar 3: Prevention: Address the root causes of youth involvement in violence and strengthen youth resilience to radicalization. Lead Institution(s): Ministry of Youth and Sports Ministry of Health Supporting Institutions: Ministry of Gender, Children and Social Protection Ministry of Education Ministry of Internal Affairs Ministry of Finance and Development Planning Liberia Peacebuilding Office Community-Based Organizations (CBOs) CSOs UN agencies NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 64 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Representing the voices of the youth and the civil society, the Federation of Liberian Youth (FLY) and the West Africa Network for Peacebuilding (WANEP), as Co-Chairs of the Coalition, will lead the Coalition's daily operations, including organizing meetings and follow-ups on the NAP implementation process. They will represent the Coalition at key stakeholder forums and report directly to the Coalition Chair and the Global Coalition on Youth, Peace, and Security. Meanwhile, to ensure effective action, the Coalition has established task forces dedicated to: 1.Monitoring, Evaluation, and Reporting 2.Programmes and Planning 3.Communications and Advocacy 4.Resource Mobilization NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 65 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 MONITORING AND EVALUATION FRAMEWORK NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 66 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 The implementation of the National Action Plan on Youth, Peace, and Security (NAP YPS) will be guided by a robust Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) Framework that not only tracks output but also consistently measures long-term societal impact. Recognizing that tracking output alone does not demonstrate meaningful change, this framework adopts the Vital Signs Framework, a tested and adaptable model for measuring peace and development outcomes across diverse contexts. Core Impact Areas To ensure that all proposed and future activities contribute to a shared vision of progress, the M&E Framework will track a standard set of impact indicators across all interventions. These indicators reflect the lived experiences of young people and the broader societal shifts the NAP aims to influence: 1.Agency: The extent to which young people feel empowered to make decisions and influence change in their communities. 2.Institutional Legitimacy: The responsiveness of institutions to youth needs and the level of trust young people place in these institutions. 3.Intergenerational Trust: The quality of relationships and collaboration between youth and older generations, especially in intergenerational initiatives. 4.Sense of Safety: Young people’s perceptions of physical, emotional, and psychological safety in their environments. 5.Resources (Human and Capital): The availability and accessibility of resources that support youth-led peace and security efforts. Reporting and Evaluation Cycle Quarterly Updates: Implementing institutions (coalition members, CSOs, and government actors) will submit brief progress updates every quarter. Biannual Reports: More detailed reports will be submitted twice a year, capturing both output-level data and progress on the core impact indicators. Annual Report: The Secretariat will compile and submit a comprehensive Annual Report to the Ministry of Youth and Sports, synthesizing all updates and reports. Mid-Term Evaluation: At the end of Year

text: of physical, emotional, and psychological safety in their environments. 5.Resources (Human and Capital): The availability and accessibility of resources that support youth-led peace and security efforts. Reporting and Evaluation Cycle Quarterly Updates: Implementing institutions (coalition members, CSOs, and government actors) will submit brief progress updates every quarter. Biannual Reports: More detailed reports will be submitted twice a year, capturing both output-level data and progress on the core impact indicators. Annual Report: The Secretariat will compile and submit a comprehensive Annual Report to the Ministry of Youth and Sports, synthesizing all updates and reports. Mid-Term Evaluation: At the end of Year 2, an independent external evaluator will conduct a mid-term review, assessing progress against the five core impact areas. Detailed Terms of Reference (ToR) will be developed and advertised before this evaluation. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 67 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 By embedding the Vital Signs Framework into the M&E process, this approach ensures that all stakeholders-especially young people-can see how their contributions are advancing the goals of the NAP. It also provides a consistent, evidence-based picture of the NAP’s societal impact over the 3–5-year implementation period. The implementation of this National Action Plan on Youth Peace and Security will be tracked through a clear and concise Monitoring and Evaluation Plan as defined below: Written progress updates will be provided quarterly by various implementing institutions (coalition members and other CSOs, including government interventions). In contrast, progress reports will be made biannually (coalition members and other CSOs, including government interventions). The M/E framework will track how young men and women's sense of agency, Institutional Legitimacy Intergenerational Trust, Young people's understanding of safety, and Resources (human and capital) have improved. At the end of each budget and program year, the secretariat will collate progress updates and bi-annual reports, submitting a comprehensive Annual Report on the implementation of the NAP YPS to the Ministry of Youth and Sports. Additionally, at the end of two years, there will be a mid-term evaluation of the implementation by an external evaluator, who will track progress against outcomes. Before the mid-term review, a detailed Terms of Reference will be developed and advertised. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 68 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 RESOURCE MOBILIZATION STRATEGY & ADVOCACY AND COMMUNICATION STRATEGY NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 69 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 The effective implementation of the Liberia National Action Plan on YPS requires adequate financial resources. This strategy outlines key approaches to mobilizing resources from national, international, and private sources, ensuring sustainable funding for youth-led peacebuilding initiatives. Below are the sources from which funding could be mobilized: National Budget Allocation/Funding The Government of Liberia should prioritize YPS within its national budgeting framework by: Establishing dedicated budget lines for YPS programmes within relevant government ministries and agencies (e.g., Ministry of Youth and Sports, Ministry of Internal Affairs, Ministry of Gender, Children, and Social Protection, Ministry of Education, Ministry of Justice. Mainstreaming YPS funding into existing national development programmes and ensuring cross-ministerial collaboration for co-funding opportunities. Establishing an Inter-Ministerial Funding Committee. International Partnerships and Donor Engagement Collaboration with international development partners, including UN agencies, regional organizations, and bilateral donors, can help secure funding through: Aligning YPS priorities with donor strategies and seeking program-based funding. Establishing multi-donor trust funds for YPS projects. Leveraging technical assistance and in-kind contributions from international partners. Recommended Steps Establishment of a joint programme for the NAP implementation by UN agencies. Development of a joint Government and

text: Gender, Children, and Social Protection, Ministry of Education, Ministry of Justice. Mainstreaming YPS funding into existing national development programmes and ensuring cross-ministerial collaboration for co-funding opportunities. Establishing an Inter-Ministerial Funding Committee. International Partnerships and Donor Engagement Collaboration with international development partners, including UN agencies, regional organizations, and bilateral donors, can help secure funding through: Aligning YPS priorities with donor strategies and seeking program-based funding. Establishing multi-donor trust funds for YPS projects. Leveraging technical assistance and in-kind contributions from international partners. Recommended Steps Establishment of a joint programme for the NAP implementation by UN agencies. Development of a joint Government and Partners national resource mobilization framework to fund NAP interventions. Institute measures to access and identify youth-related funding mechanisms enshrined in the Corporate Social Responsibilities of concessions agreements. Engage the Private Sector and Philanthropic Institutions to diversify funding sources to ensure the following: Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) initiatives, focusing on youth employment, peacebuilding, and social cohesion. Private sector funding for youth-led peace initiatives and leadership programs. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 70 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Development and implementation of innovative financing models such as social impact bonds and challenge grants to support YPS projects. Youth-Friendly Funding Mechanisms Ensure direct access to funding for youth-led organizations by: Creating small grant schemes with simplified application and reporting processes. Establishing flexible funding mechanisms that prioritize grassroots and unregistered youth groups. Providing capacity-building support to enhance youth organizations' ability to manage funds effectively. Recommended Steps Establish a youth trust fund Seek legislative approval of a percentage of the Consolidated Account to fund the NAP YPS Organize NAP YPS annual fundraising program at Annual YPS Summit. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 71 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Advocacy and Communication Strategy Key Activities Conduct targeted advocacy campaigns to secure political support and resource commitments Engage key decision-makers, including the Legislators and government officials, through policy briefs and high-level dialogues. Showcase success stories and impact through public communication channels to attract more investment in YPS initiatives. Recommended Steps Development of a NAP Communication Strategy Publication of a quarterly NAP newsletter Monthly YPS media engagements Expansion of NAP social media engagements Seeking sponsorship for NAP media engagements Monitoring and Accountability To ensure transparency and efficiency in resource utilization, there will be: Implementation of budget tracking and financial audits for YPS programs. Establishment of participatory monitoring frameworks involving youth representatives. Regular publication of reports on YPS funding allocations and outcomes. NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 72 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 COSTING FRAMEWORK NATIONAL ACTION PLAN ON YOUTH, PEACE AND SECURITY 2025-2030 REPUBLIC OF LIBERIA | PAGE 73 NATIONAL FRAMEWORK/ACTION ON UNSCR 2250 Costed Action Plan The costed action plan outlines the proposed funding required to implement critical priorities of the NAP YPS. It is presented based on the six priority areas, their related activities, cost estimates, responsible entities, and the timeframe for achieving the activities. These six priority areas were determined by participants drawn from various youth institutions, CSOs, and government institutions based on the key challenges faced by young people in Liberia, identified during both the consultation exercises and the Design Workshop. text: Youth, Peace and Security, Finland's National Action Plan 2021–2024: Mid-term report to Parliament on the implementation of the Action Plan UNOFFICIAL TRANSLATION BY THE FINNISH YOUTH COUNCIL & YOUTH SECTOR (FYCS), FUNDED BY THE EUROPEAN UNION UN Security Council Resolution 2250 "Youth, Peace and Security" highlights the role of youth in conflict prevention and resolution and other peace and security issues. In 2021, Finland became the first country in the world to publish a National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security for 2021–2024 and has been a pioneer in implementing the resolution. The mid-term report prepared by the Ministry for Foreign Affairs for the Parliament's Foreign Affairs Committee gives an overview of the implementation of the Action Plan during its first two years. The mid-term report consists of reports submitted by the responsible ministries and organisations to the Ministry for Foreign Affairs. The aim of the report is to give an overview of how the Youth, Peace and Security work has started in Finland. Table of contents 1. Introduction 3 1.1 Finland's National Action Plan 3 1.2 Finland's pioneering role in the Youth, Peace and Security agenda 4 2. Implementation 4 2.1 National implementation 5 2.1.1 Ministries and central government 5 2.1.2 NGOs 6 2.2 International implementation 6 2.2.1 Ministries and central government 7 2.2.2 NGOs 8 Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. Mid-term report to Parliament on the implementation of the Action Plan Youth, Peace and Security work supports the objectives of Finland's foreign and security policy and development cooperation. The work promotes the building of peaceful societies, strengthens Finland's international partnerships and aims to strengthen and develop a multilateral system. The Youth, Peace and Security agenda is of growing importance in building peaceful and sustainable societies, particularly in Africa and the Middle East. Young people's contribution is also needed to address global security issues such as climate change. Based on the information in the mid-term report, the implementation of the Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security is well underway. The Action Plan has been implemented by the organisations and the government in all its priority areas, both nationally and internationally. Several dozen measures have been reported. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has placed particular emphasis on the involvement of young people in peace processes, which is also one of the priorities of the Foreign Ministry's Centre for Peace Mediation. Other ministries have focused more strongly on peace and security issues in youth work and have strengthened youth participation. The work of these ministries emphasises the integration of the elements of the Action Plan into existing broader strategies and projects concerning young people and peace and security. Finnish organisations are actively working on Youth, Peace and Security and bring significant added value to its implementation. They also coordinate their work partly through their own networks. The 2250 network, coordinated by the National Youth Council Allianssi and UN Youth of Finland, plays an important role in bringing together the organisations implementing the Action Plan and individual citizens. The Action Plan has strengthened cooperation between public authorities, young people and the NGO sector, and the development of mechanisms to support youth participation. The Finnish model of cooperation between authorities and young people has been particularly effective in international contexts, where it has helped to facilitate dialogue between parties that would not otherwise meet. The implementation of the Action Plan has promoted youth participation in peace and security issues, provided support and tools for ministries and organisations to work with young people, and been a visible part of Finland's international profile, for example at the UN

text: and individual citizens. The Action Plan has strengthened cooperation between public authorities, young people and the NGO sector, and the development of mechanisms to support youth participation. The Finnish model of cooperation between authorities and young people has been particularly effective in international contexts, where it has helped to facilitate dialogue between parties that would not otherwise meet. The implementation of the Action Plan has promoted youth participation in peace and security issues, provided support and tools for ministries and organisations to work with young people, and been a visible part of Finland's international profile, for example at the UN and bilaterally. It is important to ensure that sufficient resources are available to continue to successfully implement the agenda and maintain Finland's high profile. Finland's participatory approach to developing a national action plan has attracted a lot of interest and there has been a demand for Finnish expertise. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs and youth organisations have actively shared Finnish experiences and good practices. There is a demand for Finnish involvement in the international Youth, Peace and Security arena. This supports Finland in building international partnerships. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has coordinated the national implementation of the Action Plan. The monitoring group, coordinated by the Ministry for Foreign Affairs, plays an important role in supporting implementation, facilitating mainstreaming and providing a forum for communication between actors. In spring 2023, the Ministry for Foreign Affairs strengthened the human resources for Youth, Peace and Security. The current Action Plan will expire at the end of 2024. In many respects, the objectives and measures set out in the Action Plan will still be relevant at the end of 2024. Updating the Action Plan to reflect the changed foreign and security policy situation would enable the coherent continuation of the work on Youth, Peace and Security in Finland beyond 2024. Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. 1. Introduction UN Security Council Resolution 2250 "Youth, Peace and Security", adopted in 2015, and subsequent Resolutions 2419 (2018) and 2535 (2020) recognise the positive and active role of youth in conflict prevention and resolution, peacebuilding and the prevention of violent extremism. Member States should take into account the needs and perspectives of young people and increase their participation in decision-making and all stages of peace processes. The resolution is based on the idea that inclusive, equal, democratic and human rights-based social structures support sustainable peacebuilding. The meaningful participation of different social groups, including young people, in peace processes makes them more sustainable. The Youth, Peace and Security resolution was initiated by Finnish youth activists who presented a petition to UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon in Finland in 2011. The starting points for Finland's international Youth, Peace and Security work are linked to the objectives of foreign and security policy and development cooperation. The work contributes to the UN 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and its goal to promote peaceful societies (Goal 16). The work also has links to the rights of women and girls and achieving gender equality (Goal 5). Youth, Peace and Security work demonstrates Finland's commitment to building inclusive peace and sustainable societies as part of the UN Security Council agenda, brings new partnerships for Finland and is part of Finland's efforts to develop and strengthen the multilateral system. Finland has been active in the Youth, Peace and Security theme in bilateral relations and in international organisations and has shared its experiences in working on the Action Plan. Finland has also actively involved Finnish youth in international 2250 cooperation, thus supporting the development of young people's skills. Finland has been an

text: girls and achieving gender equality (Goal 5). Youth, Peace and Security work demonstrates Finland's commitment to building inclusive peace and sustainable societies as part of the UN Security Council agenda, brings new partnerships for Finland and is part of Finland's efforts to develop and strengthen the multilateral system. Finland has been active in the Youth, Peace and Security theme in bilateral relations and in international organisations and has shared its experiences in working on the Action Plan. Finland has also actively involved Finnish youth in international 2250 cooperation, thus supporting the development of young people's skills. Finland has been an active player in the UN, the EU and in its relations with the African Union. In the future, Finland has the potential to raise its profile in this area also in NATO. The Youth, Peace and Security theme is also an integral part of Finland's Africa strategy, and the agenda has been actively implemented in contacts with African countries. At national level, the implementation of the Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security adds value to efforts promoting the position and wellbeing of youth in a variety of ways, including by focusing on youth participation in society, partnership development, media literacy, inclusiveness, integration and work against exclusion, violence and extremism, thus also supporting the objectives of Prime Minister Petteri Orpo's Government Programme. It has also increased coordination and cooperation between government actors and youth organisations. 1.1 Finland's National Action Plan Finland's National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security was published in August 2021, making it the first in the world. It provides tools for authorities and organisations to better recognise the specific and important role of young people in peace and security issues. The Action Plan has been prepared in cooperation with young people and youth organisations, central government, research institutes and civil society. It has five priorities: participation; prevention; partnerships; protection; and disarmament, demobilisation and reintegration. Cross-cutting themes are technology and climate change. The goals and actions apply to Finland's actions both at home and globally. The aim is to involve young people from diverse backgrounds and abilities. Internationally, Finland is seen as having considerable expertise in the preparation of action plans on Youth, Peace and Security. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs is responsible for the national coordination and monitoring of the Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security and for the operation of the monitoring group. The cross-administrative monitoring group, composed of different actors, meets 2–4 times a year. The 2250 network for young people plays a key role in monitoring and implementing the Action Plan and in lobbying for the objectives of the agenda. The implementation of the Action Plan is reported to Parliament at the mid-term and end of the programme period. Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. The current Action Plan expires at the end of 2024. The final report on the implementation of the Action Plan will be published in 2025. On the basis of the mid-term report, it can be estimated that many of the goals and actions set out in the Action Plan are still relevant at the end of 2024. Updating the Action Plan to reflect the changes in the foreign and security policy situation would enable the coherent continuation of Finland's Youth, Peace and Security work beyond 2024. 1.2 Finland's pioneering role in the Youth, Peace and Security agenda Finland has a strong international image as a pioneer of the Youth, Peace and Security agenda. This stems from several years of consistent work to promote the agenda and Finland's inclusive and civil society-oriented approach to

text: can be estimated that many of the goals and actions set out in the Action Plan are still relevant at the end of 2024. Updating the Action Plan to reflect the changes in the foreign and security policy situation would enable the coherent continuation of Finland's Youth, Peace and Security work beyond 2024. 1.2 Finland's pioneering role in the Youth, Peace and Security agenda Finland has a strong international image as a pioneer of the Youth, Peace and Security agenda. This stems from several years of consistent work to promote the agenda and Finland's inclusive and civil society-oriented approach to preparing our national agenda. Finland's experience and expertise have attracted a lot of international interest. People want to hear about and learn from the Finnish model. Finland has actively shared its experience in its work on the Action Plan and the implementation of the Youth, Peace and Security agenda. This work has been carried out by the Ministry for Foreign Affairs and youth organisations, both together and individually. The Finnish model has been presented in bilateral contexts, at events organised by international organisations (EU, OSCE, UN) and at civil society events in Finland and abroad. Experiences of cooperation between the Ministry for Foreign Affairs and the NGO sector have been good. NGOs, Finnish embassies and the Ministry for Foreign Affairs all reach different actors and bringing them together is meaningful in itself. Cooperation with youth organisations strengthens the skills and capacity of young Finns to act on international issues. Youth, Peace and Security work is relatively new and there are not yet many active players at national level. However, interest in the agenda has been growing strongly. In Africa, national action plans and strategies are being developed in several countries. There is also growing interest in the Middle East, and the Arab League is in the process of publishing its own Youth, Peace and Security strategy. Finland is not a major funder of this action plan work but is a sought-after player for its strong intellectual capital and credible national action. The Youth, Peace and Security agenda is therefore a natural channel for Finland to seek and strengthen partnerships with countries working on the agenda. Until now, Finland has carried out its own work on Youth, Peace and Security with relatively few financial and human resources, often as part of other preparation work by public officials and without a separate budget. Through consistent, long-term efforts, with the involvement of the NGO sector, Finland has become a prominent player in the field of Youth, Peace and Security. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has strengthened the resourcing of the Youth, Peace and Security work by establishing the post of Youth, Peace and Security Officer in spring 2023. The implementation of the Action Plan requires cross-administrative cooperation within the central government and therefore the resources allocated to this work should be examined across all branches of government. Adequate resourcing is a prerequisite for Finland to continue to maintain its international reputation while benefiting from the work already done. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs plays an important role in promoting the 2250 agenda, coordinating national activities and maintaining Finland's international profile. 2. Implementation The Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security is implemented by organisations and central government at national and international level. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs coordinates the activities. The mid-term report consists of reporting produced by the key ministries responsible for the work and by the organisations in the summer of 2023. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs plays an important role in promoting the 2250 agenda, coordinating national activities and maintaining Finland's international profile. 2. Implementation The Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security is implemented by organisations and central government at national and international level. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs coordinates the activities. The mid-term report consists of reporting produced by the key ministries responsible for the work and by the organisations in the summer of 2023. The following actors submitted responses by the deadline: the Ministry for Foreign Affairs, the Ministry of Defence and the Finnish Defence Forces, the Ministry of Social Affairs and Health, the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment, the Ministry of Justice, the Ministry of the Environment, the Ministry of the Interior and its Crisis Management Centre Finland and the Ministry of Education and Culture. On the civil society side, reporting was provided by the Guides and Scouts of Finland, CMI – Martti Ahtisaari Peace Foundation, Finn Church Aid, YMCA Finland, Finnish Evangelical Lutheran Mission, the United Nations Association of Finland, the Department of Geographical and Historical Studies at the University of Eastern Finland, Finnish National Youth Council Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. Allianssi, UN Youth of Finland and Demo Finland. Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. 2.1 National implementation The national measures in the Action Plan are linked to young people's wellbeing, opportunities for participation and preventative work. In ministries (excl. the Ministry for Foreign Affairs) and smaller organisations, the agenda is actively implemented at national level. The priorities for national work focus on the following objectives: 1: participation, 2: prevention and 4: protection. The implementation of the Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security supports the efforts to combat threats to the security of young people and to prevent radicalisation. The Action Plan identifies education, employment opportunities, prevention of exclusion and strengthening social inclusion as important factors in preventing radicalisation and crime. Measures emphasise participation through training, peace education, consultation and inclusiveness. Measures also focus on preventing the spread of misinformation and developing young people's media literacy. 2.1.1 Ministries and central government The key contribution of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs to national measures has been the national coordination of the Youth, Peace and Security work and the implementation of the Action Plan in the branch of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs. Together with the monitoring group, the Ministry for Foreign Affairs has drawn up guidelines for the authorities to support the national implementation of the Action Plan and the meaningful participation of young people. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has also reinforced connections with youth organisations. Cooperation with the Finnish National Youth Council Allianssi and the 2250 network is key. Guidelines to support the implementation of Youth, Peace and Security have been drawn up and training has been organised for the missions of the Finnish Foreign Service. The Finnish National Youth Council Allianssi has coordinated and developed youth delegate activities with various ministries. The links of the delegate programmes with the national work on Youth, Peace and Security in Finland have been strengthened. A youth delegate participates in international meetings as part of the Finnish delegation and monitors their ministry- or sector-specific area for a period of two years. This activity supports the participation of young people in international processes. The youth delegate programme has proven to be an effective model which should be further developed and the meaningful participation of the delegates strengthened. Ministries have created more ways in which young people can be meaningfully involved in decision-making

text: activities with various ministries. The links of the delegate programmes with the national work on Youth, Peace and Security in Finland have been strengthened. A youth delegate participates in international meetings as part of the Finnish delegation and monitors their ministry- or sector-specific area for a period of two years. This activity supports the participation of young people in international processes. The youth delegate programme has proven to be an effective model which should be further developed and the meaningful participation of the delegates strengthened. Ministries have created more ways in which young people can be meaningfully involved in decision-making and have their voices better heard, for example through youth delegate programmes, expert consultations and youth panels. Young people's views are being increasingly heard in the decision-making process. The monitoring of the effectiveness of youth consultation needs to be further developed. The ministries' actions focus on seminars and training for young people to strengthen peace work and global education, and on training on Youth, Peace and Security more broadly, for example as part of civilian crisis management. Youth, Peace and Security issues have also been more widely integrated into the Women, Peace and Security training (CMC, Ministry of Defence, Defence Forces). Special attention has been paid to the protection of young people and the prevention of exclusion (Ministry of the Interior). Efforts are also being made to combat discrimination and racism and to create safe spaces for young people, but less attention is being paid to the active role of young people in these themes. Preventive work and the development of media literacy are strongly present in the activities of the ministries (Ministry of Education and Culture). As part of the implementation, particular attention has been paid to the specific integration needs of young people. The Finnish Immigration Service has organised training for reception staff and the reception legislation has been reformed to take better account of the best interests of the child (Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment, Ministry of the Interior). In addition, guidelines and tools have been developed for preventing, identifying and addressing bullying, violence, harassment and discrimination against children and young people (Ministry of Social Affairs and Health). In several ministries, Youth, Peace and Security work has supported many projects and strategies Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. concerning children and young people that are already ongoing. These include the National Child Strategy, the implementation of the Lanzarote Convention, the Non-Violent Childhoods Action Plan and the National Youth Work and Youth Policy Programme. Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. Although the Youth, Peace and Security agenda is well known, ministry representatives would like more support in mainstreaming youth security issues and concrete tips on how to implement it. Inter-ministerial brainstorming and leveraging synergies in the implementation of the agenda are also welcomed. Example 2.1.2 NGOs In their implementation of the Action Plan, NGOs have paid particular attention to developing young people's expertise and meaningful participation. This work has supported young people's wellbeing, for example through psychosocial support. Young experts representing organisations have brought young people's perspectives to central government working groups, consultations and preparation. The organisations have provided global education and leadership training for young people and supported the development of network members as advocates. These activities have aimed to prevent the marginalisation of young people, to promote their skills and expertise in the peace and security sector and to promote their views in policy-making. At national level, the organisations need support particularly in terms of ensuring the continuity of work and evaluating their activities. National Youth Council

text: supported young people's wellbeing, for example through psychosocial support. Young experts representing organisations have brought young people's perspectives to central government working groups, consultations and preparation. The organisations have provided global education and leadership training for young people and supported the development of network members as advocates. These activities have aimed to prevent the marginalisation of young people, to promote their skills and expertise in the peace and security sector and to promote their views in policy-making. At national level, the organisations need support particularly in terms of ensuring the continuity of work and evaluating their activities. National Youth Council Allianssi has played a major role in Finland's national Youth, Peace and Security work. Allianssi has acted as a key link between the central government and the youth organisations. In 2022, Allianssi strengthened its work on Youth, Peace and Security with additional funding from the Ministry for Foreign Affairs. With this grant, Allianssi coordinated and developed the Finnish 2250 network. The grant strengthened the impact of the network's activities and increased public awareness of youth peace work and the themes of resolution 2250. Example 2.2 International implementation The international implementation of the Action Plan has been promoted in particular by the Ministry for Foreign Affairs, Finland’s missions and the biggest NGOs. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs and Finnish organisations have supported the preparation of national action plans on Youth, Peace and Security Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. around the world by organising training and workshops on the Finnish Action Plan and its participatory development model. Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. The development of action plans has been supported particularly on the African continent. Nigeria and Kenya are strong players, but there is also interest in the agenda from Somalia, Ethiopia, Senegal, South Africa, Namibia, Mozambique and others. Regional actors in Africa such as the AU and ECOWAS have also taken an active approach to the agenda. Finnish actors are also working on the 2250 agenda in Central and South America, where the most active actors are Mexico, Colombia and Chile, and in the Middle East, where the most active countries are Jordan, Iraq, Lebanon and Palestine. 2.2.1 Ministries and central government Through the Action Plan, the Ministry for Foreign Affairs has developed approaches for engaging more consistently with young people on peace and security issues. Supporting meaningful youth participation in peace processes is one of the priorities of the Centre for Peace Mediation of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has also actively promoted this agenda in both bilateral and multilateral cooperation. At the UN, Finland has actively promoted the Youth, Peace and Security agenda as part of the UN General Assembly negotiations on the establishment of the UN Youth Office. Finland has seconded a youth expert to support the work of the Office of the UN Secretary-General's Envoy on Youth. This has supported the coherent promotion and development of the Youth, Peace and Security theme as part of UN’s broader youth work. In January 2022, Finland supported and participated at a high level in the International Symposium on Youth Participation in Peace Processes, organised by Qatar and the UN. Discussions with young people and their meaningful participation in peace processes have been a regular feature of the mediation meetings organised by the Ministry for Foreign Affairs. In 2022, Minister for Foreign Affairs Pekka Haavisto appointed 29-year-old MP Suldaan Said Ahmed as his Special Representative on Peace Mediation in the Horn of Africa. In his work, Said Ahmed has placed particular emphasis on the inclusion of

text: as part of UN’s broader youth work. In January 2022, Finland supported and participated at a high level in the International Symposium on Youth Participation in Peace Processes, organised by Qatar and the UN. Discussions with young people and their meaningful participation in peace processes have been a regular feature of the mediation meetings organised by the Ministry for Foreign Affairs. In 2022, Minister for Foreign Affairs Pekka Haavisto appointed 29-year-old MP Suldaan Said Ahmed as his Special Representative on Peace Mediation in the Horn of Africa. In his work, Said Ahmed has placed particular emphasis on the inclusion of youth in peace processes, meeting with local young peacebuilders and bringing their views to the forefront of formal peace processes. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has consistently supported dialogue between young people and decision-makers and the inclusion of young people's views in policy debates. Youth issues featured prominently at the EU-African Union Summit in February 2022, where Finland co-organised a side event on Youth, Peace and Security. A discussion between young people and decision-makers was also organised in the context of the Nordic-African Foreign Ministers' meeting in Helsinki in June 2022. The work on youth participation supports the development of Finnish youth's skills and capacities to engage in international contexts and strengthens international networking. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has supported the preparation of national actions plans on Youth, Peace and Security by actively disseminating information on Finland's National Action Plan and its participatory preparation model to partner countries. In November 2022, the Helsinki Workshop, organised together with the Search for Common Ground and UNOY organisations, brought together government actors and young people from Africa, the Middle East, Asia and Europe working on the Youth Peace and Security agenda. With a total of 34 participants from 14 countries and three international organisations (OSCE, EU, UNDP), the event brought together actors from different regions in a unique way and has received a lot of positive feedback. The Ministry for Foreign Affairs has kept the 2250 agenda on the table in bilateral political discussions. The Youth, Peace and Security agenda has been discussed in policy dialogues with South Africa, Mexico, Colombia and Kenya, among others, at the ambassadorial, ministerial and head of state level. In the Letter of Intent on Co-Operation in Peace Mediation signed during the President's state visit to South Africa in April 2023 between the Finnish and South African Foreign Ministries, young peace mediators play a central role. Finnish embassies implement Finland's National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security both bilaterally and by supporting international and local organisations. In Africa, Finland has provided strong support to the national action plan processes on Youth, Peace and Security, especially in Nigeria and Kenya. The embassies also work as part of the EU front in their respective countries. In autumn 2022, the European Commission and the European External Action Service (EEAS) published the Youth Action Plan (YAP) in EU External Action for 2022–2027, which aims to build Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. strategic partnerships with young people around the world. One of the themes of the YAP is Youth, Peace and Security. Finland was involved in contributing to the content of the YAP. Finland has also promoted the inclusion of the Youth, Peace and Security agenda in EU civilian crisis management. However, the promotion of the agenda in EU cooperation between Member States in Brussels still requires further input. However, the promotion of the agenda in EU cooperation between Member States in Brussels still requires further input. Example 2.2.2 NGOs Finnish organisations support youth participation in peace processes and political decision-making through training, a leadership development programme, mentoring programmes, development cooperation projects and global education. They promote networking among young peace mediators and help raise the voice of young people in local government. Finnish organisations and members of the 2250 network for young people have promoted the Youth, Peace and Security agenda at their events. Examples of such activities include a side event organised with the Search for Common Ground organisation during Africa-Europe Week in February 2022 and a workshop on Youth, Peace and Security organised with YMCA Europe and MIJARC at the European Youth Forum. The National Youth Council Allianssi coordinated a study trip to the European Parliament for members of the 2250 network, actively participated in meetings of the global and EU-level Youth, Peace and Security Coalition and shared experiences from the process of preparing the Finnish National Action Plan with youth organisations in international contexts. Allianssi also launched the Future Agents NOW project with the National Youth Councils of Estonia and Denmark. The 2250 network, which has been working with the agenda for a long time, plays an important role in bringing together the organisations and individual citizens implementing the Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security. The network is currently co-ordinated by Allianssi and UN Youth of Finland. The organisations have also intensified their cooperation on Youth, Peace and Security issues. YMCA Finland, CMI – Martti Ahtisaari Peace Foundation, Finn Church Aid and Finnish Evangelical Lutheran Mission have formed the Youth and Peace Network to support work on the Youth, Peace and Security agenda. Training and projects within these measures are often linked to broader issues such as democracy education, human rights and sustainable development, which support foreign and security policy priorities. Youth, Peace and Security activities are often implemented with development cooperation funding. They are also strongly linked to broader equality issues such as the inclusion of women and people with disabilities. Example Translation of this document was funded by the European Union. Experiences of cooperation between organisations and Finnish embassies have been positive and this cooperation will be continued and strengthened in the future. The guidelines for Finnish embassies on the implementation of the Finnish National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security encourage them to make better use of cooperation with local and Finnish organisations. This will also reinforce the objective of Prime Minister Petteri Orpo's Government Programme to intensify cooperation with the Finnish NGO sector, especially in terms of peace mediation and development cooperation. Strategy‘s Goals and Objectives Goal To promote and institutionalize youth participation in peacebuilding processes in South Sudan by facilitating a National Youth, Peace and Security Strategy designed by young women and men Specific Objectives To increase youth engagement in peace processes, contributing to the formulation and implementation of sustainable peace agreements at national and community level To enhance gender equality and social inclusion, recognizing the vital roles young women and men play in shaping South Sudan’s post-conflict trajectory 4 Section 2 The Policy Framework Section 2. source_name: Nigeria National Action Plan on Youth Peace and Security
text: National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 1 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security PREFACE In most conict situations around the world, the youths are always considered the perpetrators as well as victims. They are either active in the frontline or they are at the receiving end of the aftermath of war. Owing to this, the UNSCR 2250 resolution became necessary for countries all over the world to adopt and provide spaces for youth to contribute to the entire efforts of preventing violence and ensuring sustainable peace in communities globally. Nigeria, which is also a member of the global community and a UN member-state thought it necessary to work together with other peace seeking nations of the world by adopting the UNSCR 2250 and domesticating all its tenets through a Youth Peace and Security (YPS) National Action Plan (NAP). Herewith, is a delineation of a substantial background to the process of adopting the UNSCR 2250 for the implementation of the Nigerian Youth Peace and Security National Action Plan; the document is hoped to be adopted by the 36 States of the federation and the FCT. Submissions according to the six pillars 2 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security presented in the UNSCR 2250 from the several consultations both locally, regionally, and nationally are documented to suggest the directions and dimensions the YPS NAP will take during implementation. Leveraging on the energy deployed by the youth during the recent EndSARS protest all over the country, it becomes necessary that the youths, through the YPS NAP are empowered to actively participate in peacebuilding and nation-building. The YPS National Action Plan is a pilot programme which will span for three years, that is, 2021 to 2024 after which it can be reviewed. Mid-way into its implementation, there will be a review of the entire action plan to ascertain the level of progress made so far. Major stakeholders responsible for driving the implementation of the action plan are outlined while the implementation strategy extensively discussed considering outputs based on the pillars, priority activities to be carried out throughout the implementation period, key performance indicators to measure the expected outcomes derived from the strategic objectives, while the lead and other actors involved in the YPS 3 National Action Plan are reected. As a work-in-progress document, the National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security is the rst of its kind in the continent, seeking the support of all stakeholders locally and internationally for its implementation. 4 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security FOREWORD Youth are one of the most important segments of any society, and are, signicantly, the largest percentage of any country's workforce and most active population across the globe. The Nigeria's Youth too are not only vibrant and energetic but play key role in our national development efforts such that we cannot allow their energies and innovations to rust away. Unfortunately, many dwell more on their ip side: always associated with vices such as: restiveness, violence, crimes, insecurity, etc. Their vulnerability, therefore, placed them in a vantage position as both victims and perpetrators of these vices. The AU Charter (2006), identifying the roles of youth in Peace and Security, therefore calls for youth participation in the promotion of sustainable peace and security in Africa. Furthermore, the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) at its 73rd meeting on 9 December, 2015 adopted United Nation Security Council Resolution (UNSCR) 2250, which recognizes the need for youth to be actively engaged in ensuring peace as well as contributing to justice and reconciliation. This is the rst international legal and political

source_name: Nigeria National Action Plan on Youth Peace and Security
text: crimes, insecurity, etc. Their vulnerability, therefore, placed them in a vantage position as both victims and perpetrators of these vices. The AU Charter (2006), identifying the roles of youth in Peace and Security, therefore calls for youth participation in the promotion of sustainable peace and security in Africa. Furthermore, the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) at its 73rd meeting on 9 December, 2015 adopted United Nation Security Council Resolution (UNSCR) 2250, which recognizes the need for youth to be actively engaged in ensuring peace as well as contributing to justice and reconciliation. This is the rst international legal and political framework to recognize the 5 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security impact of armed conicts on youth as well as their role in peace and security. This implies that with inclusive policies in place, the large youth population can present a unique demographic dividend, capable of contributing to lasting peace and economic prosperity. Noting this reality, the Council urged Member States to domesticate the resolution by engaging communities, non-governmental organizations and other relevant stakeholders to develop strategies to counter violent extremist ideas and terrorism. Furthermore, Member States are implored to increase youth representation in decision-making at all levels, as a way of addressing the threat to stability and development posed by the rise in radicalization among young people. In Nigeria, to fully embrace and domesticate UNSCR 2250, a Youth, Peace and Security (YPS), National Action Plan (NAP) is required to spell out the mechanisms for meaningful participation of young people in maintaining peace and security. The YPS NAP is to identify roles expected of relevant stakeholders such as: Ministries, Departments and Agencies (MDAs), Civil Society Organizations (CSOs) and private organizations, concerned with peace, security and 6 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security development. The YPS NAP is designed to complement existing national, regional and international policy initiatives, and to reect Nigeria's commitment to ensuring the meaningful engagement of youth in conict prevention, post-conict and peace building efforts. The YPS NAP is also intended to serve as a useful guide to Federal, State and Local Governments, as well as other non-governmental stakeholders, to ensure that their various programmes address youth needs in all the pillars of UNSCR 2250, which are: Participation, Protection, Prevention, Partnership, Disengagement and Reintegration. Since 2016, the collaboration between the Federal Ministry of Youth and Sports Development (FMYSD), Institute for Peace and Conict Resolution (IPCR), Centre for Sustainable Development and Education in Africa (CSDEA), Voluntary Youth Organizations (VYOs) and other Civil Society Organizations (CSOs) embarked on series of rigorous meetings, conferences and consultations with several other stakeholders at communities, states and national levels, to harvest their inputs which was validated and later afrmed on 27th August, 2021, 7 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security leading to the development of the YPS NAP document. The YPS NAP was eventually launched on 1st November, 2021, during the National Youth Day celebration, by President Muhammadu Buhari (GCFR). The implementation strategies are well spelt out in the document, and the exercise is expected to cut across all the 774 Local Government areas of the country in phases, starting with pilot states, one each from the six geo-political zones. The successful launch of YPS NAP is another milestone in Nigeria, particularly as it relates to youth involvement in maintaining peace and security. At this juncture, all hands must be on deck to ensure full implementation of the National Action Plan on YPS, for the transformation of Nigeria into a stable, peaceful, virile, economically prosperous and developed country. Mr. Sunday Dare Honourable Minister Federal Ministry of Youth and

The United Nations Security Council (UNSC) at its 73rd meeting, on 9 December, 2015 adopted UNSC Resolution 2250, which recognizes that youth should be actively engaged in shaping lasting peace and contributing to justice and reconciliation. The United Nations (UN) encouraged member states to domesticate and ensure full implementation of the UNSCR 2250 on Youth, Peace and Security; hence, in swift response, some youth inclined Civil Society Organizations (CSOs), and the Institute for Peace and Conict Resolution (IPCR), in collaboration with Federal Ministry of Youth and Sports Development worked assiduously to develop a Youth, Peace and Security National Action Plan (YPS NAP) for Nigeria, after crisscrossing the nooks and crannies of this country, for 10 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security necessary inputs. The document which is to serve as a guiding principle and working tool for the implementation of the UNSCR 2250 on YPS in Nigeria has been successfully put together by what is now known as the Nigeria Coalition on Youth, Peace and Security who deserve commendation and worth mentioning here, amongst the host of others. We have to rst of all, express our profound gratitude to God Almighty, who granted the enablement, inspiration and protection to all who have contributed to the success of this document. We hereby express our indebtedness to President Muhammadu Buhari (GCFR) for his full support and eventual and gracious launch of the National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security. Also worthy of our appreciation is the Honourable Minister of Youth and Sports Development, Mr. Sunday Dare for his uninching support and encouragement. To the United Nations Population Fund, the European Union, 11 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security the African Union, the Global Civil Society Platform for Peacebuilding and Statebuilding, the Global Coalition on Youth, Peace and Security, CORDAID, the African Union, we say a big thank you for the support enjoyed from your ofces. The efforts and technical support of the Institute for Peace and Conict Resolution (IPCR) towards the accomplishment of this YPS NAP cannot go unnoticed; and particularly to the Director General, Professor Bakut Tswah Bakut of the Institute, we are grateful. The technical and huge nancial support enjoyed from the Centre for

text: the European Union, 11 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security the African Union, the Global Civil Society Platform for Peacebuilding and Statebuilding, the Global Coalition on Youth, Peace and Security, CORDAID, the African Union, we say a big thank you for the support enjoyed from your ofces. The efforts and technical support of the Institute for Peace and Conict Resolution (IPCR) towards the accomplishment of this YPS NAP cannot go unnoticed; and particularly to the Director General, Professor Bakut Tswah Bakut of the Institute, we are grateful. The technical and huge nancial support enjoyed from the Centre for Sustainable Development and Education in Africa (CSDEA) is highly commendable; especially the immense contribution of its Executive Director, Mr. Theophilus Ekpon, cannot be over-emphasized. The uninching support of the Nigeria Coalition on Youth, Peace and Security, who sacriced their time, energy and resources to the success of the document, is commendable; while our appreciation also goes to other Civil Society Organizations (CSOs), youth groups 12 National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security and other notable individuals from nooks and crannies of this country, which space would not permit me to mention here. Meanwhile, the untiring support of the Acting Director, Hajia Amina Mohammed Dauran, and the effort of all the staff of the Network and Social Mobilization (NSM) Department of the FMYSD, especially the following: Dayo Bamigboye, Despan Kwardem, Adedoyin Oyekan, Lami Bature, Charles Victor, Isaac Adeagbo and Vivian Iorzua who were involved in this project, are also worth commendation. Let me conclude at this juncture that what has been achieved so far is just the rst phase of the project. Therefore, it is now a clarion call on all and sundry that all hands must be on deck, to ensure the full implementation of the UNSC Resolution 2250 as enunciated in the National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security, which is the second and the most important phase of this program.
