Human Security: freedom from fear (fear of conflict, violence and crime); freedom from want (poverty, infectious diseases and environment degradation) and freedom from indignity (discrimination and humiliation). 2 Negative Peace: the absence of physical violence including war.3 NEET: youth Not in Education, Employment, or Training , a vulnerable category to various social, economic, and cultural issues Participation: a pillar of UNSCR 2250 which emphasizes the creation of opportunities for the youth to be recognised and represented in decision-making levels at all levels (local, national, regional and international) on issues that directly affect their well-being including conflict prevention and resolution. Partnership: a pillar of UNSCR 2250 focusing on the establishment of relevant relationships that strengthen efforts on issues affecting the youth. Peace: The absence of physical and structural violence and the presence of mutually harmonious relationships including justice, equality and safety.4 Peacebuilding: various initiatives meant to strengthen relationships and rebuild communities in order to prevent the re-occurrence of conflicts.5 Positive Peace: entails the attitudes, institutions and structures that underpin and sustain peaceful societies.6 Prevention: a pillar of UNSCR 2250 that calls on governments to implement violence prevention programs and also create avenues for the positive contribution of youth to peacebuilding activities. 1 Ramsbotham, et. al (2011) 2 Inter-American Institute of Human Rights, (n.d) 3 Galtung (1967: 6) 4 KROC Institute for International peace studies (n.d); Anderson (2004) 5 Tschirgi (1996) 6 Galtung (1964) vii Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Protection: a pillar of UNSCR 2250 that supports activities that reduce exposure of the youth to various forms of violence. It also calls for the conviction of those who may have committed various crimes against young people. Security: refers to the state of being free from dangers, crises or threats arising from various sources. (Buzan,1999: pp. 432).7 Structural violence: a combination of extreme inequality, social exclusion and humiliation of people’s dignity resulting from political, social and cultural processes and institutions. UNSCR 2250 National Action Plan: a plan to domesticate and implement UNSCR on youth, peace and security. UNSCR 2419:

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: 2024 Protection: a pillar of UNSCR 2250 that supports activities that reduce exposure of the youth to various forms of violence. It also calls for the conviction of those who may have committed various crimes against young people. Security: refers to the state of being free from dangers, crises or threats arising from various sources. (Buzan,1999: pp. 432).7 Structural violence: a combination of extreme inequality, social exclusion and humiliation of people’s dignity resulting from political, social and cultural processes and institutions. UNSCR 2250 National Action Plan: a plan to domesticate and implement UNSCR on youth, peace and security. UNSCR 2419: emphasises the role that young people can play in the implementation of peace agreements and also in conflict resolution. UNSCR 2535: focuses on the ‘critical role of the youth in prevention and resolution of conflicts.’ It also reaffirms commitments made under UNSCR 2250 of 2015 and UNSCR 2419 of 2018. Youth: those aged between 10 to 35 years in Malawi. Youth, Peace and Security Agenda: a program that recognises the critical role that the youth can play as active change agents and participants in Peace and Security initiatives and structures even at the local level. Violence: the lack of human security and the presence of chaos and instability. Violent Extremism: extreme beliefs and actions of people that use or support the use of violence to achieve ideological, religious and political goals.8 7 Buzan (1991) 8 Australian Government (2014) viii Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 1.0 Introduction This National Action Plan on UNSCR 2250 presents priority actions for Malawi to enhance the role of the youth in peace and security. It identifies the key drivers of violence by and against young people, as well as factors constraining their participation in enhancing peace and security. It presents the priority actions to address these in key focus areas that are aligned to four of the five pillars of the UNSCR 2250, namely: participation, protection, prevention, and partnerships. An implementation plan and a monitoring and evaluation plan are attached as annexes to the National Action Plan. 1.1 Youth, Peace and Security Agenda The Youth, Peace and Security Agenda is guided by a set of three United Nations Security Council Resolutions (UNSCR) that mark a shift in the understanding of who young people are and their role in peace and security. The landmark UNSCR 2250 was adopted by the United Nations Security Council in 2015. It provides a global policy framework that explores the following questions: (i) how conflict impacts young people’s lives; (ii) what must be done to mitigate its effects; and (iii) how the youth can be meaningfully engaged in creating peaceful communities. The resolution has five main pillars, namely: participation, protection, prevention, partnerships, and disengagement and reintegration. These provide guidelines to member-states in the development of policies and programs to mitigate the impact of violent conflicts on young people and to meaningfully engage them in managing conflict, peace processes and creation of peaceful communities. The first pillar, Participation, obliges member-states to increase the participation of young people in decision making at all levels and in mechanisms for the prevention and resolution of conflict. The second pillar, Protection, requires member states to ensure the protection of civilians, especially young people, in times of conflict and postconflict, including protection from all forms of sexual and gender-based violence. The third pillar, Prevention, requires member-states to support youth engagement through creation of spaces which recognize and support young people to implement violence prevention activities. This includes creation of policies that support the positive contribution of young people to peace

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: first pillar, Participation, obliges member-states to increase the participation of young people in decision making at all levels and in mechanisms for the prevention and resolution of conflict. The second pillar, Protection, requires member states to ensure the protection of civilians, especially young people, in times of conflict and postconflict, including protection from all forms of sexual and gender-based violence. The third pillar, Prevention, requires member-states to support youth engagement through creation of spaces which recognize and support young people to implement violence prevention activities. This includes creation of policies that support the positive contribution of young people to peace building efforts and establishment, by all relevant actors, of mechanisms to promote a culture of peace and to discourage young people from all acts of violence. The fourth pillar, Partnerships, puts an obligation on member-states governments to strengthen partnerships with relevant actors by among others, increasing support to the work of UN bodies that are engaged in promoting peace, development, and equality, as well as engaging and empowering community actors and local people, including the youth in countering violent extremism and promoting social cohesion and inclusion. While not applicable for Malawi, the fifth and final pillar, Disengagement and Re-integration, encourages all actors involved in planning disarmament, demobilization and re-integration (DDR), to achieve sustainable peace, to consider the impact of these processes on the youth as well as the needs of the youth affected by the conflict. 1 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Subsequent resolutions to UNSCR 2250 include UNSCR 2419 of 2018 which focus on the positive roles of the youth in negotiating and implementing peace agreements and conflict prevention. The UNSCR 2419 calls on stakeholders to facilitate full participation of young people in decision making processes related to peace. Some of the key elements of UNSCR 2419 include, regular reporting on Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) to UN; protection of civic and political spaces of young people and the active role of the youth in responding to humanitarian needs.9 The other subsequent resolution is UNSCR 2535 which recognises the youth as active participants in conflict prevention and resolution. The three resolutions together place emphasis on the active role that the youth can play in conflict resolution, peace building, and prevention of violent extremism. 1.2 National Action Plans for UNSCR 2250 National Action Plans for UNSCR 2250 have been developed as a way of domesticating the resolution at country level. The plans provide entry points for youth-responsive peace building at country level and can leverage important regional and district leadership and networks to inform overall strategy and shape programmatic activities. Furthermore, localisation of the resolution with better documentation of good practices and operational learning is needed in terms of what may work or what may not work and how feasible it is to replicate or upscale particular efforts. The UNSCR 2250 has so far been domesticated in Finland and Nigeria. Several countries are in the process of developing the NAPs. This Malawi NAP is developed to domesticate UNSCR 2250. 1.3 Peace and Conflicts in Malawi Malawi is considered a peaceful country despite having underlying conflict dynamics and socio- economic risks and vulnerabilities that threaten social cohesion, community security, and peaceful co-existence. Tensions have mainly arisen over contestation on adherence to the rule of law and the principle of separation of powers, personalization of political power, land and chieftaincy duels, and political transitions among others. These have shaken the national fabric. The evolving democratic culture as experienced since transition from one party state to democracy in 1994, has portrayed that conflict is inherent in

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: is developed to domesticate UNSCR 2250. 1.3 Peace and Conflicts in Malawi Malawi is considered a peaceful country despite having underlying conflict dynamics and socio- economic risks and vulnerabilities that threaten social cohesion, community security, and peaceful co-existence. Tensions have mainly arisen over contestation on adherence to the rule of law and the principle of separation of powers, personalization of political power, land and chieftaincy duels, and political transitions among others. These have shaken the national fabric. The evolving democratic culture as experienced since transition from one party state to democracy in 1994, has portrayed that conflict is inherent in the national political, legal and socio-economic environment, which result in high-stakes elections. There have been stalemates in parliament between ruling party and opposition. These include among others lack of intraparty democracy, uncoordinated dialogue outside parliament, political intolerance, political leadership rivalries, and transition challenges have characterised the political parties until recently. Other pertinent structural issues worth highlighting include religious related disagreements, gender-based violence and electoral 9 Global coalition on Youth, Peace and Security (2022) 2 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 violence. For instance, Malawi is composed of 77.3% Christians and 13.8 % Muslims and has not been spared from religious tensions.10 Inter-religious tensions, particularly in the political context, has acted as an occasional conflict trigger in Malawian society since independence.11 There have been also instances where Rastafarian children have experienced exclusion from attending schools due to their dreadlocked hair. The foregoing indicates that religious tensions remain salient in Malawi’s social fabric. Likewise, gender-based violence is a common occurrence in Malawi and it manifests itself through sexual abuse, rape, domestic violence and also economic deprivation. The 2022 Afrobarometer survey reveals that most Malawians perceive gender-based violence as a priority issue. One in 5 women have ever experienced sexual violence in their lifetime.12 There are also emerging narratives of gender-based violence cases against men. In 2021, 1778 abuses against men were recorded between January and July of 2021 in Malawi.13 It is a common feature that young women and men have been negatively impacted by GBV incidences. A Malawi Police Service (MPS) Victim Support Unit report of 2023 indicates that defilement cases is second largest form of violence after physical abuse.14 This calls for the need to protect the victims especially the adolescent girls and young women (AGYW) in line with the protection pillar of the UNSCR 2250. Malawi has been privileged to have six successive peaceful elections with high voter turnout since the advent of multiparty politics in 1994. While the 1994 and 2009 general elections are considered peaceful, the 1999, 2004, 2014 and 2019 elections have been tainted with violence arising from dissatisfaction with results and electoral management. The 2019 elections stand out as an election with high recorded incidents of violent conflict that point out structural drivers of electoral violence. The campaign period witnessed creation of no go zones for political opponents, burning of vehicles and offices that in some instances resulted in deaths. The post- election period protests against results turned violent with property looting and damage which remain unaccounted for to date. Police are considered to have heavy-handedly reacted during crowd control which resulted in clashes between the police and protesters leading to the death of a police officer in Lilongwe. The 2019 post-electoral violence unearthed Malawi’s susceptibility to electoral violence and the associated social and economic costs, some of which cannot be quantified. Three main factors have been identified as the reasons for electoral violence: zero-sum nature of Malawi’s Political competition; electoral systems and deficiency in Malawi’s electoral Commission (MEC)

text: deaths. The post- election period protests against results turned violent with property looting and damage which remain unaccounted for to date. Police are considered to have heavy-handedly reacted during crowd control which resulted in clashes between the police and protesters leading to the death of a police officer in Lilongwe. The 2019 post-electoral violence unearthed Malawi’s susceptibility to electoral violence and the associated social and economic costs, some of which cannot be quantified. Three main factors have been identified as the reasons for electoral violence: zero-sum nature of Malawi’s Political competition; electoral systems and deficiency in Malawi’s electoral Commission (MEC) and radicalization of political party youth wings.15 Some young people are used by political elites to incite violence against opponents. Political party youth wings from both ruling and opposition political parties have been involved in violence, especially during the election period. This implies that the youth have been incited to create violence which is an antithesis of peace. Malawi has a 10 2018 Malawi Population and Housing Census 11 Development of a national Peace Architecture for Malawi September 2013 12 Chunga & Nedi (2022) 13 Chirwa (2021) 14 MPS Victim Support Unit Administrative Data 2023 15 Hussein & Sambo (2020) 3 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 vast recruiting constituency for perpetrators of electoral violence given the high numbers of youth underemployment and youth not in education, employment, or training (NEET). From the cases of electoral violence reported by the media in the last five general elections, it is conspicuous that older and senior politicians engage young people to perpetrate violence on their opponents. The youth wings of political parties serve as instruments of terror and violence especially for parties in power. The major reason for this is that youth NEET in Malawi is very high. The youth policy recognizes that young people in the NEET category are vulnerable to various social, economic, and cultural issues. Many idle but energetic youth easily sign up as supporters of the parties and as potential agents of political violence for token amounts of money that neo-patrimonial patrons generate and channel to young people.16 Cynical observers of the Malawian youth argued that “political violence is the only useful activity that Malawi’s political leaders have identified for the youth’s country”. 1.4 Youth, Peace and Conflicts in Malawi Young people make up the largest growing proportion of Malawi’s 20 million plus population. The Malawi 2018 Population and Housing Census estimated that 51% of the population is below 18 years old. When the age bracket is stretched to 18-34 years, the percentage population of youth becomes a significant majority.17 This implies that the youth form a large part of the population, hence, their voice is crucial in ensuring that there is sustainable peace that is trans-generational. The youth also account for 20% of the population that is poorest and at risk of experiencing various social and economic shocks. The youth in Malawi face a number of challenges that limit their participation in conflict prevention and resolution mechanisms. On the other hand, there are also challenges that push them into being perpetrators of violence. Young people in Malawi are also venerable in times of violent conflicts. These constitute constraints, which strategies in National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security will have to target in order to turn the young people into agents of peace other than violence perpetrators. The constraints faced by young people in this regard include social, economic and political exclusion including high levels of unemployment and underemployment; radicalization of political party youth wings; increasing rural-urban migration of unemployed

text: in conflict prevention and resolution mechanisms. On the other hand, there are also challenges that push them into being perpetrators of violence. Young people in Malawi are also venerable in times of violent conflicts. These constitute constraints, which strategies in National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security will have to target in order to turn the young people into agents of peace other than violence perpetrators. The constraints faced by young people in this regard include social, economic and political exclusion including high levels of unemployment and underemployment; radicalization of political party youth wings; increasing rural-urban migration of unemployed young people; rising cases of domestic and sexual violence; limited opportunities for youth participation in mechanisms for prevention and resolution of conflicts; the absence of integrative institutions as a breeding ground for factionalism; and threat of young people involvement in violent extremism. (i) Social, economic and political exclusion Young people in Malawi face social, economic and political exclusion as evidenced by their limited participation in political governance; failure to fulfil their socially expected gender roles, such as property ownership in the case of young men, failure to gain status and access to resources due to gender norms in case of young women due to prevailing gender norms; and lack of employment 16 Chingaipe (2017) Malawi in Bargiacchi et al (eds) Preventing and Mitigating Electoral Conflict and Violence 17 Benson et al (2019) 4 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 opportunities. These factors tend to drive young people into violence as a way of venting their frustration against their exclusion. The Youth in Malawi grow up in a socio-economic environment where society has prescribed expectations on what roles young men are expected to play as they grow up into adulthood, including marriage, getting gainful employment and accumulating private property. Whereas failure to fulfil such roles is caused by a number of factors including limited employment opportunities, low levels of education attained and lack of access to effective and quality education, most young men that find themselves in such situations turn to violence as means of venting their anger and expressing their dissatisfaction. Similarly, whereas society has also prescribed roles that young women must perform as they drift into adulthood, most such roles work against young women’s aspirations for accumulation of property, access to resources and achievement of high status. In the absence of any redress mechanism against their situation, such young women may engage in violence as a way of challenging the predominant gender norms. The recent spike in gender based violence against men may be due to this factor. Regarding economic exclusion, most young people in Malawi experience unemployment and underemployment. Eighty-three (83%) of Malawian youth work in informal sectors and 82% are underqualified for jobs.18 Further, although efforts have been made, through previous interventions, to promote economic empowerment by increasing their participation in TEVET to encourage self-employment, the number of young people that are NEET or underemployed remain high. Challenges that remain include lack of entrepreneurial culture among most young people and lack of access to affordable credit facilities to most young people who are still needy. Further, opportunities for skills and entrepreneurial training have not grown at a pace fast enough to absorb the growing number of secondary school graduates and drop-outs. This leaves a large number of idle young people who are prone to engage in violence either because they have been merely enticed by older people who have specific motives to achieve through violence perpetration, or because they are operating under the influence of drugs and alcohol, which they consume

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: lack of entrepreneurial culture among most young people and lack of access to affordable credit facilities to most young people who are still needy. Further, opportunities for skills and entrepreneurial training have not grown at a pace fast enough to absorb the growing number of secondary school graduates and drop-outs. This leaves a large number of idle young people who are prone to engage in violence either because they have been merely enticed by older people who have specific motives to achieve through violence perpetration, or because they are operating under the influence of drugs and alcohol, which they consume and abuse due to their idleness. Young people in Malawi are excluded from participation in political governance, due to among others, the prevailing culture that politics is the domain of adults whose role in society is to be leaders and make decisions; lack of civic education on the importance young people’s participation in political governance and their duties as citizens; and lack of trust in political governance structures and officials (Mbulaje et al, 2021). As a result of this, young people rarely gain representation in decision-making forums like Parliament, District Councils, and Cabinet as their participation in political governance is limited to campaigning for their favourite candidates and voting during elections. This leaves them prone to enticement by older politicians who would like to use violence to gain political mileage. 18 OECD (2022) Youth-wellbeing a review of Malawi: A 60 seconds guide. 5 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 (ii) Radicalization of political party youth wings Since most young people are excluded from effective and constructive political participation as discussed above, they are maintained in political parties by older politicians to incite political violence against their opponents. All political parties in Malawi have youth wings that are employed to intimidate political rivals, destabilize opponents and disrupt their political rallies to fulfill the political motives of the older politicians. This way, young people also become victims of political violence perpetrated by young people from rival parties. At the intra-party level, young people also perpetrate violent conflict against each other on behalf of competing candidates. (iii) Increasing youth rural-urban migration A relative lack of economic opportunities for young people in rural areas triggers the ever-increasing rural-urban migration of this demographic group. These young people, therefore, migrate from rural to urban areas in search of economic opportunities including education, employment, and entrepreneurship. This increases the population of young people who are unemployed, financially unstable, and susceptible to involvement in violent activities. A study by Mbulaje et al (2021, 8), revealed that many young people who live in shanty towns in Malawi are financially unstable as the majority of them are either students or job seekers. (iv) Rising domestic and sexual violence Domestic and sexual violence is rampant in Malawian families, especially targeted at women, including young women, mainly due to a predominantly patriarchal orientation of Malawian society (Malawi Government 2017, 40). Cases of gender-based violence frequently reported to be happening at family level include physical battering, rape, child sexual abuse, early marriages, especially for girls. According to the Demographic Health Survey Report of 2015 - 2016, 34% of women have experienced physical violence since the age of 15, and 20% have experienced sexual violence. Of late there has been a surge in cases of domestic violence targeting men, including young men, which often go unreported due to cultural beliefs. This seems to be a deliberate effort by the female gender to fight the predominant gender norms through violent behaviours. Adequate mechanisms need to be put in place

text: to be happening at family level include physical battering, rape, child sexual abuse, early marriages, especially for girls. According to the Demographic Health Survey Report of 2015 - 2016, 34% of women have experienced physical violence since the age of 15, and 20% have experienced sexual violence. Of late there has been a surge in cases of domestic violence targeting men, including young men, which often go unreported due to cultural beliefs. This seems to be a deliberate effort by the female gender to fight the predominant gender norms through violent behaviours. Adequate mechanisms need to be put in place to deter these vices and protect the victims from the effects thereof. (v) Youth participation in the prevention and resolution of conflicts In the recent past, there has been limited representation of young people in mechanisms for the prevention and resolution of conflicts. This has meant that the country’s peace initiatives had left out the largest share of the country’s population in decision-making and implementation of initiatives that are meant to benefit them. The government recognized this gap when designing the country’s National Peace Architecture which recommended the inclusion of young people in structures established to promote prevention and resolution of conflicts. Under the National Peace Architecture initiative, the Malawi Peace and Unity Commission (MPUC) has been established, and Commissioners have since been appointed including a representative of the youth. District 6 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Peace and Unity Committees (DPUCs) will be established in all local government areas, and will also include a representatives of the youth in the committees. Currently, such committees were established only in 6 districts, and they have been operational since 2016 and 2018 on a pilot basis. Despite having various youth participation structures, challenges remain in that most young people lack capacities and skills in conflict prevention and resolution mechanisms that would make them effective agents of peace. Young people currently serving in the existing DPUCs have benefited from government and development partner capacity-building initiatives but some of them may not qualify for reappointment when the MPUC reconstitutes them either because they will have transitioned into adulthood and may not meet other appointment criteria in line with the Peace and Unity Act, 2022, which was enacted and came into force at least four years after their appointment to serve in the pilots. Further, limited engagement with their constituencies has meant that the knowledge and skills acquired had not been shared with their peers to create a general awareness of conflict prevention and resolution among the youth constituency in their respective districts. In addition, youth-adult partnership as well as partnerships and networking among youth organizations and peace-building agencies at both national and district levels do not seem to be a common occurrence. This deters young people that have taken up the role of being agents of peace from realizing the benefits of partnerships and networking with experienced agencies, which include knowledge and skills acquisition as well as complementarity of efforts. (vi) Youth and violent extremism Studies dating back to the 1980s suggest that a vast majority of recruits into terrorist and other extremist organizations are young people between the ages of 15 and 29, largely from a low to middle-income backgrounds (USAID 2005, 5). Young people facing unemployment, political exclusion, and abuse have nothing to lose by joining extremist groups. With the status of the youth in Malawi as highlighted above, there is currently a potential threat for radicalisation. This stems from the rising wave of extremist groups in the Sub-Saharan region. The recent spike in violent

(vi) Youth and violent extremism Studies dating back to the 1980s suggest that a vast majority of recruits into terrorist and other extremist organizations are young people between the ages of 15 and 29, largely from a low to middle-income backgrounds (USAID 2005, 5). Young people facing unemployment, political exclusion, and abuse have nothing to lose by joining extremist groups. With the status of the youth in Malawi as highlighted above, there is currently a potential threat for radicalisation. This stems from the rising wave of extremist groups in the Sub-Saharan region. The recent spike in violent extremism in neighbouring Mozambique creates a potential risk to Malawian youth who may get exposed to extremist ideologies through direct and indirect recruitment into groups such as the Al-Sunnah-Wa-l-Jama’ah (ASWJ) who are operating in Cabo Delgado. The acts of violent extremism in Cabo Delgado can have both direct and spill-over effects, especially on the Southern Malawi such as the influx of refugees and the radicalization and recruitment of marginalized youth especially the NEET into the insurgent group. (vii) Youth, Civic and Peace Education While Malawi has made efforts to incorporate civic education into its school curriculum to mold a non-violent citizenry, there is lack of a robust integrated peace education themes into various subjects to promote values such as tolerance, respect for diversity, conflict resolution, and human rights that are accompanied by responsibility. 7 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Ideally, peace education is not supposed to be limited to the formal education system but should also extend to out-of-school youth in community-based initiatives and youth clubs. Community- based organizations, religious institutions, and local leaders play significant roles in promoting peacebuilding and conflict resolution skills among community members. While religious and traditional leaders have played an active role in this area, their contribution has been reactive, rather than proactive. Enhancing peace education in Malawi requires continued commitment from the government, civil society, and international partners. Investing in capacity building for youth clubs and youth networks, teacher training, curriculum development, and community engagement can help strengthen peace education efforts and contribute to building a more peaceful and cohesive society in Malawi. Similar efforts should also be extended to tertiary education where the curriculum should incorporate peace education and specialty courses in violent conflict, peace mediation, conflict sensitivity, peace journalism, and peacebuilding. 1.5 Rationale for the National Action Plan Youth in Malawi make up a significant portion of the population. Their participation in peacebuilding efforts can help ensure that the peace achieved is inclusive and representative of all segments of society. A NAP specifically targeting youth and peace can address challenges the youth face and provide avenues for the youth to positively contribute to conflict prevention and peacebuilding, at the same time protect young people who are vulnerable to violent conflict. A NAP will also ensure the youth promote sustainable peace. Involving young people in peacebuilding processes is crucial for promoting sustainable peace. Young people represent a valuable resource for Malawi’s development. By engaging them in peacebuilding activities, the country can harness their energy, creativity, and innovation to address conflict-related issues and contribute to the overall development of the nation. Overall, a National Action Plan on youth and peace in Malawi is essential for promoting social cohesion, preventing conflict, and fostering sustainable development in the country. It represents an investment in the future of Malawi by empowering its youth to become active agents of positive change in their communities. 1.6 Linkages with Existing Policy Documents and Legal Instruments The National Action Plan on UNSCR 2250 aligns

Overall, a National Action Plan on youth and peace in Malawi is essential for promoting social cohesion, preventing conflict, and fostering sustainable development in the country. It represents an investment in the future of Malawi by empowering its youth to become active agents of positive change in their communities. 1.6 Linkages with Existing Policy Documents and Legal Instruments The National Action Plan on UNSCR 2250 aligns with various local policy documents, legal instruments, and international protocols, a few of which are outlined in this section. The NAP 2250 will strengthen youth inclusion in governance systems and transformative leadership as espoused by National Youth Policy.19 The policy further recognizes the youth as a ‘human resource potential that can contribute to national development.’ 20 The policy provides for youth participation in the grassroots structures of the National Peace Architecture, specifically the District Peace and Unity 19 Ministry of Youth and Sports (2023) 20 Ministry of Youth and Sports (2023) 8 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Committee. The NAP on UNSCR 2250, therefore, will mobilize young people to actively contribute to the peace architecture in Malawi in line with the objectives of the National Peace Policy and the Peace and Unity Act 2022 (CAP 5:2) (CAP 7:2). The National Peace and Unity Act and the Malawi Peace Policy remain the overarching policy framework for a credible national peacebuilding agenda. One of the key objectives of the Peace policy is to support efforts of building sustainable peace, and prevention of conflict through various mechanisms. Additionally, the NAP supports ideals enshrined in the Constitution of the Republic of Malawi, especially the right to equal political and economic participation. Respect for human and economic rights is conducive to the attainment of positive peace.21 The NAP is also linked to the Penal Code, particularly Amendment Act 8 of 2023 which addresses issues of recruitment and radicalisation into violent extremism. The NAP on YPS contributes to the aspirations of Malawi 2063 which envisions a Malawi that is ‘inclusively wealthy and a self-reliant nation’.22 Malawi 2063 aspires to maintain a peaceful Malawi that attracts and retains investors. At the center of this vision is the involvement of the youth by harnessing their human capital while also addressing challenges that hinder the active participation of the youth in political and economic systems. The Malawi 2063 also highlights strategies for maintaining peaceful co-existence and sustaining peace and security.23 Regionally, the NAP UNSCR 2250 echoes the African Union continental framework on Youth, Peace, and Security and the African Charter on Human Rights. One of the priorities of the African Union 2063 agenda is to silence guns and end all civil and various forms of violence committed against persons. Youth empowerment is one of the key aspirations of the African Union 2063 agenda that also links with the sustainable development goals. The African Union Peace and Security Council has set targets for its member states to develop National Action Plans as follows: 25% of its member states should develop “youth-led and centred NAP on Youth, Peace, and Security by 2024; and by 2029, 50% of its member states should have developed NAPs within the context of the continental framework on Youth, Peace, and Security.24 NAP 2250 is also aligned with the 2013 Maseru Declaration on a Framework for Peaceful Development in Southern Africa. One of the goals of the declaration is to

One of the goals of the declaration is to encourage member states to either establish or strengthen activities and structures that sustain peace and development. Internationally, NAP 2250 is aligned with Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) goal 16 on Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions, which calls for the ‘promotion of peace and inclusiveness in all societies.’ 21 Barash & Webel (2022) 22 National Planning Commission (2020) 23 see National Planning Commission (2020), pp 26, 31, 54 and 55. 24 African Union Commission (2020) 9 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 UNSCR 1325 is the only existing NAP aimed at strengthening capacities in peacebuilding and conflict management. However, the NAP on UNSCR 1325 only focuses on promoting the active participation of women in peace-building and conflict management. The NAP 2250 will, therefore, contribute to further strengthening the efforts of the UNSCR 1325 NAP in the peace-building initiatives by ensuring that Malawian youth take their place in the peace equation. 2.0 The NAP’s Strategic Outcomes This NAP identifies nine strategic outcomes that set the vision for Malawi’s Youth and Peace Agenda implementation in conflict prevention and peacebuilding. The strategic outcomes have been selected for their contribution to the four selected pillars of YPS which are participation, protection, prevention, and partnerships. The outcomes are framed to be specific, measurable, deliverable, relevant, and present focus areas where progress is expected in the next three years. The outcomes will be pursued based on evidence, and national, district, and community-level knowledge. The NAP articulates Malawi 2063 emphasis on a youth-centric approach towards sustainable development. The NAP sets out guiding principles for action, but they are not exhaustive activities that should be undertaken in order to achieve strategic outcomes. Across the strategic outcomes, the government and stakeholders will strive to ensure that initiatives take on board young people from all social backgrounds as it recognizes the heterogeneity of the youth. 10 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 2.1 Strategic Outcomes The outlined strategic outcomes serve as the key result framework in the implementation of the youth-centric interventions. Strategic Outcome 1: Decision-making Ensure meaningful participation of young people in decision-making processes at all levels of governance, including in peacebuilding initiatives, conflict resolution mechanisms, and policy formulation at national and community levels. Supporting young people’s patrticipation beyond mere representation will ensure actual involvement in decision-making at all levels, including decisions on conflict prevention and peace-building mechanisms. Young people’s meaningful participation in conflict prevention and peace-building mechanisms including mediation, dialogue, conflict prevention, and peacebuilding is essential to building sustainable peace. 13 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 However, young people in Malawi continue to be under-represented in governance at all levels of decision-making, both in formal and informal settings. Their participation in decision-making on conflict prevention and peace-building mechanisms is also limited. Challenges limiting their representation and participation in decision-making include a lack of civic awareness of the importance of young people’s participation in political governance; and a prevailing political culture of adultism, which views leadership as a preserve of older people. There is also a lack of capacity in peacebuilding and conflict resolution mechanisms; and a lack of partnerships and networking opportunities between youth-led initiatives and existing peace-building agencies, both of which limit young people’s meaningful participation in conflict prevention and peacebuilding mechanisms. This strategic outcome directly contributes to the participation pillar, and indirectly to the protection, prevention, and partnerships pillars. It will support the implementation of measures aimed at building young people’s capacities for effective participation in decision-making in

This strategic outcome directly contributes to the participation pillar, and indirectly to the protection, prevention, and partnerships pillars. It will support the implementation of measures aimed at building young people’s capacities for effective participation in decision-making in all spheres of life, including political governance and conflict resolution, and peace building mechanisms. It will also support the emergence of youth role models in political governance as a way of ending the political culture of adultism, and support the emergence of partnerships between youth-led peace building and conflict prevention initiatives and existing peace-building agencies. 14 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Youth from Nkhata-bay during a capacity building session for comprehensive sexuality education. Strategic Outcome 2: Capacity Building Enhance the capacity of young people through education, vocational training, entrepreneurship programs, and leadership development to equip them with the skills necessary for meaningful participation in peacebuilding activities. The YPS agenda is relatively young in Malawi. The youth and key stakeholders often lack the relevant capacity and knowledge to implement the agenda. Capacity building will ensure that there is ownership of the YPS agenda among the youth and address resistance and structural barriers that limit their participation in the implementation of the agenda and ability to participate in the implementation of conflict prevention and peace building interventions. Challenges include low levels of education among young people; lack of access to effective and quality education; and limited opportunities for youth employment, including lack of entrepreneurial culture among the youth. This strategic outcome directly contributes to the prevention pillar and indirectly to the partnerships pillar. It focuses on providing adequate institutional and human resource capacities to ensure that young people are viewed as equals and essential partners for peace and development. This strategic outcome will ensure a sustainable implementation of the YPS agenda through promoting collaboration with relevant stakeholders to ensure the relevance and quality of education accessed by the youth. It will also contribute to cultivation of entrepreneurial culture among young people to promote youth self-employment. It will also promote institutionalization of commitments to creating space and a conducive organizational culture, and strengthening human resources and knowledge management mechanisms in relation to YPS, including training of young people in early warning and early response, conflict analysis, mediation designing and supporting mediation process among others. 15 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Chikwawa based youth discussing gender-based conflict mitigation measures. Pic UNFPA Malawi. Strategic Outcome 3: Conflict Prevention and Resolution Strengthen mechanisms for conflict prevention and resolution by promoting youth participation in early warning and early response, dialogue, mediation, and reconciliation. The issue of conflict in Malawi is considered remote as the dominant discourse mainly supposes that conflict should be an armed one and not violent conflict. The country’s reliance on informal or ad hoc conflict resolution mechanisms, predominantly supported by faith leaders and traditional leaders as peace brokers has meant that conflict resolution and peace building remain an uncharted 16 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 area for the youth. The challenge has, therefore, been lack of inclusive mechanisms for conflict prevention and peace building that take into account the youth

text: mediation, and reconciliation. The issue of conflict in Malawi is considered remote as the dominant discourse mainly supposes that conflict should be an armed one and not violent conflict. The country’s reliance on informal or ad hoc conflict resolution mechanisms, predominantly supported by faith leaders and traditional leaders as peace brokers has meant that conflict resolution and peace building remain an uncharted 16 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 area for the youth. The challenge has, therefore, been lack of inclusive mechanisms for conflict prevention and peace building that take into account the youth perspective in the analysis of the underlying causes of conflicts and designing and implementing interventions for their resolution. Outcome three will directly contribute to the pillars of participation, prevention, protection and partnerships. It will contribute to creation of safe and neutral spaces where young people and community members feel comfortable to express their concerns and engage. This could be physical spaces like community centres or virtual spaces like online forums. It will contribute to building peaceful and resilient communities through fostering a culture of dialogue, mediation and reconciliation among the youth, and between the youth and other demographic categories. This outcome will also facilitate communication through training youth facilitators who can mediate discussions and ensure that all voices are heard. This will encourage active listening and constructive communication techniques to foster understanding and empathy; and also raise awareness about the impact of conflict on individuals and communities, and the benefits of reconciliation. The youth will be empowered to participate in conflict resolution processes and take ownership of reconciliation efforts. Efforts will be made towards encouraging youth leadership and providing opportunities for them to develop skills in conflict resolution and mediation. Outcome three will also strive to organize activities that bring together youth members from different communities that are known to be in conflict in a non-threatening environment, such as sports events, cultural exchanges, inter-political dialogue, interfaith dialogue or community service projects to enhance social cohesion, peaceful coexistence and tolerance of diversity among them. 17 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Chikwawa based youth pose for a group photograph after an interactive session on comprehensive sexuality education. Pic UNFPA Malawi. Strategic Outcome 4: Protection of Youth Rights and Well-being Ensure the protection of youth rights, including access to education, healthcare, employment opportunities and justice, while safeguarding them from exploitation and violence. Chapter IV of the Constitution of the Republic of Malawi presents a Bill of Rights that have to be respected and upheld by everyone and enjoyed equally by all persons. These include the right to personal liberty, right to education, right to economic activity, right to healthcare and freedom to enjoy political rights. The situation of the youth in Malawi as stated under 1.4 above restricts them from enjoyment of some of these rights. For example, the social, economic and political exclusion that they face, restricts their enjoyment of their right to economic activity, political rights and right to access justice and legal remedies, among others. This outcome directly contributes to the prevention, protection and participation pillars. It focuses on ensuring that government, development partners and all stakeholders are able to lay a foundation for delivering the YPS agenda by promoting access to and enjoyment by the youth of their rights. It will ensure that YPS has been integrated into justice and human rights promotion and protection in the following ways: strengthening human rights protection mechanisms in conformity with international and national legal frameworks; making human rights frameworks accessible to young people and taking

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: political rights and right to access justice and legal remedies, among others. This outcome directly contributes to the prevention, protection and participation pillars. It focuses on ensuring that government, development partners and all stakeholders are able to lay a foundation for delivering the YPS agenda by promoting access to and enjoyment by the youth of their rights. It will ensure that YPS has been integrated into justice and human rights promotion and protection in the following ways: strengthening human rights protection mechanisms in conformity with international and national legal frameworks; making human rights frameworks accessible to young people and taking specific measures to ensure the protection of young human rights defenders and peacebuilders. It will also ensure that government and stakeholders operate in a way that ensures improved access to justice for the youth and that young people are engaged in reconciliation mechanisms. 18 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 A Nkhata-bay based young woman facilitating a session on gender equality. Pic UNFPA Malawi. Strategic Outcome 5: Gender Equality Promote gender equality and empower adolescent girls and young women to actively participate in peacebuilding processes, address gender-based violence, and challenge harmful gender norms and stereotypes. The effective and accountable gender equality approach to conflict prevention is a core element of stable and peaceful societies but too often the focus is on Women and Peace as the YPS is a relatively young agenda. The situation of Malawian adolescent girls and young women, where society has prescribed roles that young women must perform as they drift into adulthood, works against young women’s aspirations for accumulation of property, access to resources and achievement of high status. The predominantly patriarchal orientation of Malawian society also contributes to domestic and sexual violence targeting women, including adolescent girls and young women. This strategic outcome directly contributes to the protection, prevention and participation pillars. It focuses on promoting adolescent girls and young women’s agency in formal and informal processes, and institutions, through capacity building, political education, and support for young women’s initiatives. It will also ensure that stakeholders adopt gender and age-responsive approaches to conflict prevention and peacebuilding. It will also ensure that government and development partners develop and implement awareness-raising programs about root causes of gender-based violence and discrimination, including reflecting on gender norms, stereotypes, and expectations related to masculinity/femininity which negatively limit the potential of young people. It will also contribute to development and implementation of programs aimed at achieving protection against sexual exploitation and abuse, prevention of genderbased violence, prevention of child marriage and programs that tackle key safety and rights issues for AGYW in particular and that help create an enabling environment for their participation. 19 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Mangochi based youth deliberating peace initiatives. Pic UNFPA Strategic Outcome 6: Youth-led Peacebuilding Initiatives Support and amplify youth-led peacebuilding initiatives, community projects, and grassroot movements that promote peace, social justice, and sustainable development. Involving young people in peace building efforts empowers them to take ownership of their future and play an active role in shaping it. Young people have the potential of bringing fresh perspectives and innovative ideas in peace building efforts. Their experiences, ideals, and creative approaches can offer new solutions to longstanding conflicts. Youth-led peace initiatives often engage with communities at the grassroots level, fostering trust, dialogue and reconciliation among diverse groups. This bottomup approach can contribute to building resilient and cohesive societies. Youth-led initiatives are also often better positioned to address the root causes of conflict, such as social injustice, inequality, and lack of opportunities, as

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: efforts empowers them to take ownership of their future and play an active role in shaping it. Young people have the potential of bringing fresh perspectives and innovative ideas in peace building efforts. Their experiences, ideals, and creative approaches can offer new solutions to longstanding conflicts. Youth-led peace initiatives often engage with communities at the grassroots level, fostering trust, dialogue and reconciliation among diverse groups. This bottomup approach can contribute to building resilient and cohesive societies. Youth-led initiatives are also often better positioned to address the root causes of conflict, such as social injustice, inequality, and lack of opportunities, as they directly experience and understand these issues. As the YPS agenda is relatively young, key stakeholders in Malawi, including different MDAs, development partners, and civil society, often lack relevant knowledge on the agenda, its principles structured around five pillars (Protection, Prevention, Participation, Partnership, and Disengagement and Reintegration), which pillars are relevant to a particular context, and the added value of investing in YPS. Most young people lack capacities and skills in conflict prevention and there is lack of partnership and networking opportunities between youth led initiatives and existing agencies in peace building and conflict resolution. This strategic outcome directly contributes to the participation, prevention and partnerships pillars. It seeks to advance knowledge on YPS through systematic advocacy efforts, targeting both strategic stakeholders as well as the broader youth and peace community of practice. It will contribute to building their confidence, leadership skills, and sense of agency in creating positive change. 20 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Youth participating in team building activities at a YouthConnekt bootcamp in Mangochi. Pic UNDP Malawi. Strategic Outcome 7: Partnerships and collaborations Ensure coherent partnerships and collaboration between the youth and government institutions, civil society organizations, academia, international agencies, and the private sector to mobilize resources, share best practices, and coordinate efforts towards youth- centered peacebuilding. As an important part of society, intergenerational dialogue, partnership, and coleadership are crucial to ensure youth meaningful participation. Young people’s participation should not be circumscribed to youth-led or youth-focused groups and organizations. All stakeholders should adopt policies and practices that support young people’s leadership and participation. By doing so, they can better enable young people to choose the form of engagement that they feel best fits their needs and goals and, at the same time, improve young people’s access to decision-making opportunities. Youth’s participation in Malawi is often limited to “Youth” issues and spaces. Young people are, therefore, not fully engaged in all larger picture societal issues and spaces. This strategic outcome directly contributes to the partnership, participation, protection and prevention pillars. It seeks to establish new and strengthen existing partnerships with young people. A broad range of stakeholders will be called upon to prioritise partnerships with youth movements and organisations and support youth-inclusive and youth-led initiatives. The strategic outcome will largely prioritize the creation of YPS coalitions to ensure a collective impact on YPS at community, regional, and national levels. Such coalitions will be multisectoral and cross-cutting partnerships between young people, including young women; youth organizations; and civil society, faith communities, private sector, religious communities, and educational institutions. 21 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 A YouthConnekt outreach event at DMI St John the Baptist University in Mangochi. Pic UNDP Malawi. Strategic Outcome 8: Research, Monitoring and Evaluation Ensure research and data-driven programming on YPS. Given the relative newness of programming for YPS, monitoring and evaluation are critically important to building the knowledge base and debunking myths about how to support and engage young people

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: coalitions will be multisectoral and cross-cutting partnerships between young people, including young women; youth organizations; and civil society, faith communities, private sector, religious communities, and educational institutions. 21 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 A YouthConnekt outreach event at DMI St John the Baptist University in Mangochi. Pic UNDP Malawi. Strategic Outcome 8: Research, Monitoring and Evaluation Ensure research and data-driven programming on YPS. Given the relative newness of programming for YPS, monitoring and evaluation are critically important to building the knowledge base and debunking myths about how to support and engage young people in peacebuilding. Building capacities of the youth and youth-led organizations in research, monitoring and evaluation shall ensure relevance, effectiveness, impact and sustainability. This strategic outcome directly contributes to the participation, prevention, protection and partnerships pillars. It will promote working with youth on monitoring and evaluation to heighten the programming transparency and accountability towards the youth, which can improve the uptake of recommendations. 22 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 A YouthConnekt outreach activity at Kamuzu University of Health Sciences, Blantyre Campus. Pic UNDP Malawi. Strategic Outcome 9: Policy Advocacy and Implementation Ensure youth mainstreaming in national development agenda, policies, and strategies, while ensuring the effective implementation of the National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security through budget allocation, institutional support, and accountability mechanisms. Malawi adopted a youth-driven long-term development agenda through the Malawi 2063 as it envisions a youth-centric inclusive wealth creating and self-reliant nation by 2063. Realizing such a vision requires that all policies and strategies developed and implemented to realize the vision are required to mainstream youth perspectives and participation to ensure that all development outcomes benefit young and adult people equally. The challenge is the limited mainstreaming of youth issues in the medium-term development frameworks, policies, and strategies, including those that would contribute to the realization of the YPS agenda in Malawi. This strategic outcome, therefore, recognizes that amidst the YPS agenda, young people have the right to play an equal role and have their perspectives factored into policies and initiatives with other actors to achieve a peacebuilding goal. It will directly contribute to the participation, prevention, protection, and partnerships pillars. Whereas measuring the success of interventions implemented to support the realization of the YPS agenda in Malawi is based on the achievement of the peace building goal, assessing the quality of the outputs of the interventions must include the role and perspectives of young women and men in that achievement. Success factors emphasize the degree of change in youth achievements, skills and confidence, political outlook, mind-set, and attitudes. This strategic outcome will, therefore, prioritize implementation of interventions that will support availing specific opportunities, resources, and skills to empower the youth so that they can advance their peacebuilding goals and participate fully in civic, economic, and political life. 23 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 3.0 NAP Implementation Arrangement How the Government and implementing partners will deliver the NAP implementation arrangement The NAP sets out the strategy for the Malawi Government, development partners, and key stakeholders working on YPS. The Ministry of Youth and Sports in collaboration with the Department of National Unity in the Ministry of Local Government, Unity, and Culture will provide overall coordination of the implementation of the NAP. In providing overall coordination in the implementation of the NAP, the Department of National Unity will closely cooperate with the Malawi Peace and Unity Commission, an independent body established by an Act of Parliament as the highest umbrella body and focal point

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: partners will deliver the NAP implementation arrangement The NAP sets out the strategy for the Malawi Government, development partners, and key stakeholders working on YPS. The Ministry of Youth and Sports in collaboration with the Department of National Unity in the Ministry of Local Government, Unity, and Culture will provide overall coordination of the implementation of the NAP. In providing overall coordination in the implementation of the NAP, the Department of National Unity will closely cooperate with the Malawi Peace and Unity Commission, an independent body established by an Act of Parliament as the highest umbrella body and focal point for promotion of unity and peace building. The Ministry of Youth and Sports and the Department of National Unity will institute a Steering Committee to be Co-Chaired by Government and UNFPA/UNDP, to be responsible for providing strategic direction in the implementation of the NAP on UNSCR 2250. Working at the national level The NAP steering committee shall consist of not more than 12 people. The Committee shall develop detailed terms of reference to guide their mandate to oversee the implementation, resource mobilisation and monitoring of the NAP. The Chairpersonship of the Committee shall be in the Ministry of Youth and Sports and the Secretariat shall be in the Ministry of Local Government, Unity and Culture. The Committee shall report to the Principal Secretary for National Unity and Principal Secretary for Youth and Sports quarterly. The Principal Secretaries will then report to their respective Ministers. The Committee shall have quarterly meetings to review implementation progress. The Department of National Unity and the Ministry of Youth and Sports will institute a national level technical committee for the implementation of the NAP, comprising members from Malawi Peace and Unity Commission, government agencies, development partners, civil society and other independent bodies involved in the implementation of the NAP. The committee will assume overall responsibility for development and implementation of the NAP 2250 strategies and work plans, monitoring achievement of expected results and reporting to the Steering Committee on achievements and challenges. The Directorate of National Unity, the Ministry of Youth and Sports and the Malawi Peace and Unity Commission will utilize their mandates to support all efforts at all levels to ensure that YPS becomes a priority for stakeholders. The Ministry of Youth and Sports and the Directorate of Peace and National Unity, under the Ministry of Local Government, Unity and Culture shall also partner with National Youth Council of Malawi; Ministry of Gender, Community Development and Social welfare; Ministry of Labour; Ministry responsible for Economic Planning and Development; Ministry of Defence; Ministry of Education; National Planning Commission; Ministry of Homeland Security; Office of the President and Cabinet; Malawi Defence Force; Malawi Police Service, Malawi Immigration, and National Intelligence Bureau and Ministry of Foreign Affairs to utilize their mandates in fulfilling the strategic outcomes for the NAP. 24 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 Working at district level At the district level the focal person will be the District Youth Officer who will work closely with the District Peace and Unity Officer and relevant structures such as the District Peace and Unity Committee, Area Peace and Unity Committee, and Youth Networks and youth clubs. The District Peace and Unity Committee will report to the MPUC on the implementation of activity plans and achievement of results. Working with partners Successful implementation of the NAP rests on strong partnership with development partners and stakeholders. Therefore, the Ministry of Youth and Sports, Department of National Unity and the MPUC will closely cooperate with development partners represented in the Steering Committee and

source_name: malawi national action plan on youth peace and security 2024 2029 2 0
text: District Youth Officer who will work closely with the District Peace and Unity Officer and relevant structures such as the District Peace and Unity Committee, Area Peace and Unity Committee, and Youth Networks and youth clubs. The District Peace and Unity Committee will report to the MPUC on the implementation of activity plans and achievement of results. Working with partners Successful implementation of the NAP rests on strong partnership with development partners and stakeholders. Therefore, the Ministry of Youth and Sports, Department of National Unity and the MPUC will closely cooperate with development partners represented in the Steering Committee and Technical Committee in the implementation of the NAP, benefiting from their technical and financial support, as well as networking and partnership opportunities in the process. All local structures most of which are under the Ministry of Local Government, Unity and Culture will also be actively involved in the implementation of this plan. These include District Councils, District Executive Committee (DEC), Traditional Authorities, District Peace and Unity Committees (DPUCs), Multi-Party Liaison Committees, Area District Peace and Unity Committees (APUCs) Area Development Committees (ADCs), Village Development Committees (VDC), Education Division Managers and District Education Managers, Area Executive Committees (AEC), and grievance redress committees. Local Organisations: NGOs; Financial institutions such as local banks,; Political Parties; institutions of higher learning; CSOs and CBOs that focus on youth initiatives; Media and Community radios; Local electoral committees; faith-based organisations; FEDOMA; and Private Sector based companies will also be engaged to provide their support and where possible financial and material support. Regional Bodies: AU, SADC, SADC Organ on Politics, Defence and Security Affairs and AFDB will also be engaged when necessary. Development Partners: UNDP, UNICEF, UNFPA, UN WOMEN, UNESCO, European Union, DFID, foreign missions and World Bank will also be included in the implementation of this NAP. 25 Malawi National Action Plan on Youth, Peace and Security 2024-2029 November, 2024 4.0 NAP Implementation Plan Strategic Outcome 1: Decision Making Ensure meaningful participation of youth in decision-making processes at all levels of governance, including in peacebuilding initiatives, conflict resolution mechanisms, and policy formulation at national and community levels. Figure 6: Summary of interlinkages between the YPS and CPS agendas • The YPS and CPS agendas are interlinked in practice. Countries most affected by climate change and fragility have comparatively large youth populations. Therefore, a greater proportion of young people globally are affected by these interlinked challenges, compared to older generations. As such, climate-related security risks disproportionately affect young people. • Although the links between youth, climate, peace and security are evident in reality, the emerging field of CPS has largely overlooked youth perspectives. • Similarly, although growing evidence shows that the effects of climate change compound existing vulnerabilities in fragile contexts, matters concerning the climate crisis have received little attention in the YPS agenda. • Despite nascent and emerging approaches toward policy integration of youth, climate, peace and security, these developments have not yet been translated into global policy recommendations. 20 PART 3: Youth perspectives on Climate, Peace and Security In this chapter, youth perspectives on climate-related By focusing on the situation as well as the perspectives security risks and integrated responses are presented.
